segments.title: Russo-Japanese War Russian victory scenario
segments.question: Could the Russo-Japanese War have gone in Russia's favor, and what peace terms would Russia and Japan have wanted?
segments.answerShort: The answer says Russia could only plausibly win with proper preparation, a capable forward fleet, and supporting infrastructure; if it did, Russia would likely seek extreme terms against Japan.
segments.summary: A Bruships 239 Q&A frames a Russian victory in the Russo-Japanese War as dependent on preparation, deployable fleet strength, and infrastructure rather than paper numbers.
segments.body: This live Q&A segment captures a compact alternate-history answer. Dr. Clarke says Russia had theoretical numbers but lacked the ability to deploy and support them effectively, then argues that a victorious Russia would push for maximal terms if it believed it could.

evidence.note: The audience prompt asks whether the Russo-Japanese War could have gone in Russia's favor and what concessions each side would seek.
The answer says a Russian victory required proper preparation, a decent forward fleet, and the infrastructure to deploy paper strength.
The answer says Russia would likely seek total conquest of Japan if it thought that was possible, while noting how difficult that would be.
